candidate_problems.text: What precise mechanism links 'generalization pressure' under uncertainty, local information, and repeated perturbation to the emergence of power laws rather than other heavy-tailed or multiscale distributions (e.g. lognormal, stretched exponential, truncated power law)? The conjecture is suggestive but does not specify necessary/sufficient conditions or discriminating predictions.

How can one operationally distinguish systems that have discovered real cross-scale invariants from systems that merely exploit predictive shortcuts that incidentally produce scale-free-looking statistics? This requires measurable criteria, intervention tests, or causal signatures separating genuine invariant-producing organization from superficial fit.

What are the boundary conditions under which adaptation instead settles on characteristic scales despite decentralization and perturbation, and how do constraints, niche stability, finite-size effects, or centralized control alter the selection dynamics? The conjecture needs a sharper phase diagram of when scale-free structure should or should not arise.
